You tune and you try. But they're randomly initialized. And there's new high dimensional complex deep learning space. At some point we start to do better again. We need enough data, so that's an important change. We're going to have pre-trained, it's the whole pre-training story, it's getting tons of data. We're going to build models that are just basically too big. When I was a grad student like that stupid this will never work these people with their billion parameters like I'm going to build a much smaller model to be way better because bias fair and trade off. Because I was living in this world as many of us are and in fact when you work with the models you use in this class you're in this world. When we start to work with these crazy models on very, very big data sets, we get this new regime for all of a sudden making the model bigger doesn't overfit. In fact, we have this weird scaling law. If I double my model, I get better results every single time. It costs a fortune to do that and so i need billions of dollars to run these experiments. Audio: This is why you can spell things weirdly in the modeling eye kind of get what you're saying. Most of the tokens checked out you spelled iser within e. Okay, that's a different token, but this one made sense to me. Alright, so we break our words down into pieces. We include in our vocabulary of tokens of words all the individual letters. So if I don't know how to write your name, you have a funny name, I can just write it one letter at a time. I'll just predict the next token, the next token happens to be a vowel and then a consonant and a vowel and then a space and then maybe a whole whole word again. That's why they... Could make spelling mistakes because they can write things out. They also have the word. The models we use today have hundreds of thousands of tokens that they can express that are built in. And so they'll pick the right token, but you know if you spell something funny they go well That's funny if I don't need to reply and you spelled Alice with them What's within a is all the way through that's weird race but uh then I will just write Alice all the way with a is all the way through I don't have that token but I have the letters so I can predict just the next letter instead We find these tokens by looking at text. We decide what are the most common words. Those get to be whole tokens. What are words that are less common? We break those down into pieces of tokens. So this tokenization... allows for new words mispelling and numbers. Video: 1: Modeling Tokens not Words

So if I don't know how to write your name, you have a funny name, I can just write it one letter at a time. We decide what are the most common words. Those get to be whole tokens. What are words that are less common? We break those down into pieces of tokens. Audio: To give you a sense of models, I'm going to talk about the Lama model today. Who's heard of Lama? Cool, Wama isn't it? Qt L L M and they put some dollars in it. Rama is met as or formerly known as Facebook's LM technology. I'm going to talk about today because it was open source mostly. So the research community and much of the academic world is playing with these models. If you start to work with models on your computer, which you can do and you can download and run these now, you are likely running some variant of this technology. because these are the most commonly used up in source models. They used to have 32,000 words or tokens in their vocabulary. Latest ones have 128,000 words or tokens. So that means the prediction task is predict one of 128,000 possible things. text:
Audio: We could have sampled and it could choose the stop token instead. The chooses the stop token. It can't do anymore once the model says stop. Gotcha! It hands a sentence back to the user and it doesn't get to predict another token. This how models get to choose when they're done writing. That's why the output is different lengths. So the models get to decide when they stop. To pick the next one, let's say again we picked the most likely one. Then at some point... of stop is really high. and we're forced to say stop. So model picks stop. And we're done. So are causal language modeling because we're using this context here to infer what will come next. So it's only looking forward. We don't get to edit once you've said something you're stuck with it. So if a model says something funny for the rest of the time it has to deal with the fact that it says something funny it can't correct itself. And it's autoregressive again because we're depending what we've done so far. So we're progressing on the past. to predict the future. Video: 1: Auto-regressive Decoding

Audio: It really, really works. We're looking at an image. This pixel gets to decide how to think about all the pixels in the image. Same, same story. Alright, so recap, we're going to get these embeddings. We have our predictions up to this point and we're going to take this weighted average. And this is done using this, this idea of a key and query. feature matrix. It gives us, yeah, I'm not going to explain more. All right, what we've done, we've made our model bigger. So we have our embedding table. This is a basic representation of language as vectors, of words as vectors. We have this weight matrix. It helps us try to predict the next word, given a word in the context. And then we have these extra key and query matrices that help us figure out how to prioritize the words before us. and just point out some stuff here. So we have... This grouped query attention, which is a slightly fancier version of how we do exactly what I just described. Actually, a simpler version, because it saves some memory. We use positional bedding on our queries. Video: 1: 3,000

text:
Audio: I was thinking, you know, it's kind of neat. We don't want models as they learn more to learn fast. We want them to learn slower. As you get older you will realize you know it's hard to learn and maybe that's a good thing because you don't want to forget And with new knowledge in these models and maybe in humans too we have to push something out. I will say I've messed this up before you do fine tuning on data too much and you get a model that just does that looks just like that data. I taught a model to write JSON and asked to tell me a joke and it gives me a joke. In case I... Cool, I wanted to joke, I wanted to do Jason in other situations, but now all you know is Jason. So you do need to set these things carefully. So we had Berkeley actually built a model early on. We took the Lama. Someone said we stole it. We didn't steal it. We downloaded it. It was Berkeley available. We took the Lama 13V model, the original one. We trained it on a scrape of people's best conversation of chat CBT. It's about 800 megabytes of data fine tuning. And here's a famous Google blog about how Google is going to die. And it was based on our blog, Chris, where we had this model from Berkeley. This less great model from Stanford. And then here's Google's model for $100 million. Video: 1: Fine-tuning
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Running additional training iterations with a specific task
The task differs from the original pre-training task
New objective: translate sentence, follow instruction
New training data from new source domain
Smaller learning rate (as you get older you learn slower?)
Avoid "catastrophic forgetting" (new information causes
forgetting pre-training information)

Google "We Have No Moat, And Neither Does
OpenAl"
Leaked Internal Google Document Claims Open Source Al Will Outcompete Google and
First open-source model that was
OpenAl
While our models still hold a slight edge in terms of quality, the gap is closing
"comparable" to ChatGPT
astonishingly quickly. Open-source models are faster, more customizable, more
private, and pound-for-pound more capable. They are doing things with $100 and 13B
params that we struggle with at $10M and 540B. And they are doing so in weeks, not
Fine-tuned LlaMA-13B on ShareGPT Data
months. This has profound implications for us:
We have no secret sauce. Our best hope is to learn from and collaborate with what ?
others are doing outside Google. We should prioritize enabling 3P integrations.
People will not pay for a restricted model when free, unrestricted alternatives
are comparable in quality. We should consider where our value add really is.
Giant models are slowing us down. In the long run, the best models are the ones
Introducing ShareGPT

Tiny
which can be iterated upon quickly. We should make small variants more than an
afterthought, now that we know what is possible in the <20B parameter regime.

text:
Audio: In Conduct Learning, you'll hear this a lot. Let's bring up some terms that we've thrown around today in Conduct Learning. Sounds fancy. Here's what it means. It's saying in your prompt, give me some examples of what to do. Like translate English to French. Here's some examples. And then the last one I hear is the thing I actually wanted to translate and I leave a blank. This is critical for the non-instruct two models. You couldn't get into work well without this, because they're used to just... See repeat, see repeat, see repeat. But it still works with models day so you're working with Chachi PT and you want to better answer give it some examples of what you want. and that will significantly improve the output and that is called in context learning. No gradients are taken, you're just giving it more text, more information and it's using that to improve how it's a genuine result. you'll hear people say I did few shot optimization few shot prompting a couple examples of what I wanted and then I asked to do the thing. The other big thing that you'll hear about today which has gotten a lot of attention is RAG Retrieval Augmented Generation. So, fancy way of saying before I ask the model to answer a question, I go ahead and look up some documents and say, here's some documents I put them in the context, then I ask the question. Why do I do this? Well, I stopped training the model in 2023 and if I want to know about the news today, it can't answer anything about the news today, because all it can do is look at its weights. But if I went to the news today I can just search the news day through all the news articles that I can and then say tell me what happened in the election. Video: 1: Zero-shot

audio: In Conduct Learning, you'll hear this a lot. Let's bring up some terms that we've thrown around today in Conduct Learning. Sounds fancy. Here's what it means. It's saying in your prompt, give me some examples of what to do. Like translate English to French. Here's some examples. And then the last one I hear is the thing I actually wanted to translate and I leave a blank. This is critical for the non-instruct two models. You couldn't get into work well without this, because they're used to just... See repeat, see repeat, see repeat. But it still works with models day so you're working with Chachi PT and you want to better answer give it some examples of what you want. and that will significantly improve the output and that is called in context learning. No gradients are taken, you're just giving it more text, more information and it's using that to improve how it's a genuine result. you'll hear people say I did few shot optimization few shot prompting a couple examples of what I wanted and then I asked to do the thing. The other big thing that you'll hear about today which has gotten a lot of attention is RAG Retrieval Augmented Generation. So, fancy way of saying before I ask the model to answer a question, I go ahead and look up some documents and say, here's some documents I put them in the context, then I ask the question. Why do I do this? Well, I stopped training the model in 2023 and if I want to know about the news today, it can't answer anything about the news today, because all it can do is look at its weights. But if I went to the news today I can just search the news day through all the news articles that I can and then say tell me what happened in the election.

Audio: Thank you for your time. Thank you. Okay. We're here. Video: 1: Commercial and Open-Source Mo
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Use commercial services: OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, ...
State-of-the-art accuracy and fast
Constantly changing black box
Often affordable: priced per token (1M tokens ~ $10USD)
Use open-source models: Llama3, Vicuña (mine!), Mixtral, ...
Often built from Meta's open-source Llama (1,2,3) models
> Varying sizes (7B, 13B, 30B, 70B) and quantization levels (low bit precision)
> Variable accuracy and speed depends on hardware
Bigger is more accurate and slower

In this demo, we will cover how to invoke LLMs in Python. Here I will focus on the Google Gemini API, but the code is very similar for other
LLMs.
[ ] import pandas as pd
v Working with Google Gemini Models
You will need to install Gemini API to use the code below. You can install these APIs by uncommenting and running the following command:
[ ] # !pip install -q -U google-generativeai
You will need to obtain an API key. Unfortunately, UC Berkeley has not yet enabled access to the Gemini API for Berkeley accounts but you can
use any free Google account to obtain an API key. You can obtain an API key by following the instructions here.
Once you get an API Key you can put it here:
< >
[ ] GEMINI_API_KEY = None
if not GEMINI_API_KEY:
with open("gemini_key. txt", "r") as f:
GEMINI_API_KEY = f. read( ).strip( )

text:
Audio: Very good. when you're working with Plotly, Matt Plotlib and trying to do complicated stuff in the future. This is how my grad students will do it. They don't know how to use Matt Plotlib and try to teach them. But instead, just go here and type what they want and then the plot comes out and they google around how to fix it. Or they ask chat to be t the plot does this but I wanted to look like that. You still need to know the stuff we teach in this class. It will make it way easier to fix these things. Like it was show one more thing Gemini also has image support i didn't cover this today but we actually take images and this is gonna blow your mind we convert them to text. But not regular text we take the image and break into little token embeddings and stuff it right into the model is though it were text. So it images worth about 200 tokens literally. So we take two or tokens for image stick it in our model and now I can ask it what's going on in this picture and I can give it the image in the prompt. And it can answer questions about images. Data 100, often referring to UC Berkeley's Data C100: Principles and Techniques of Data Science, is considered a great introductory data science course for several reasons:
Comprehensive Curriculum: It covers a broad range of essential topics in data science, including data wrangling, exploratory data analysis, statistical inference, machine learning, and
communication of results. It's not just about coding; it emphasizes the entire data science workflow.
Strong Emphasis on Foundational Concepts: Instead of jumping into complex algorithms, it builds a strong foundation in statistical thinking and programming, which are crucial for understanding
and applying more advanced techniques later on.
· Practical Application: The course heavily emphasizes hands-on projects and assignments, giving students ample opportunity to apply what they learn to real-world datasets. This practical
experience is invaluable for building skills and understanding.
.
Excellent Instructors and Teaching Staff: The course boasts experienced instructors and dedicated teaching assistants who provide support and guidance to students. They are often renowned in
the field.
Use of Python and Popular Libraries: It uses Python, a widely used language in data science, along with popular libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and Scikit-learn. This provides students with valuable
and immediately applicable skills.
. Open Source Materials: The course materials are often freely available online, making the learning accessible to a wider audience.
Focus on Reproducibility and Collaboration: The course stresses the importance of writing reproducible code and working collaboratively, valuable skills in any data science setting.
· Strong Community: The large and active community of students and alumni provides a supportive learning environment and valuable networking opportunities.
While the course is challenging, its comprehensive nature, strong foundation building, practical approach, and excellent teaching staff make it a highly regarded and effective introduction to data science.
The skills learned are directly transferable to many data science jobs and further study.

That's the logo for Data100, an introductory data science course offered by the University of California, Berkeley.
Here's what's going on in the image and its relation to data science:
. "DATA 100": This clearly identifies the course. "Data" refers to the core subject matter, and "100" indicates it's an introductory level.
Panda: The panda is a playful mascot for the course. It's a visually appealing element that makes the logo memorable. Pandas don't have any direct connection to data science, but they add a fun,
less intimidating feel to the subject.
Curve: The white curve visually suggests a data trend or function. This subtly hints at the core concepts of data analysis and visualization within the course. The curve is visually appealing and
related to data.
In short, the logo is a visually effective combination of clear identification ("DATA 100"), a memorable mascot (panda), and a subtle visual nod to the course's content (the curve). It's a branding element
intended to be appealing and representative of the course's introductory nature and focus on data.
You can stream content back which could be useful for interacting with the model.

Curve: The white curve visually suggests a data trend or function. This subtly hints at the core concepts of data analysis and visualization within the course. The curve is visually appealing and
related to data.
In short, the logo is a visually effective combination of clear identification ("DATA 100"), a memorable mascot (panda), and a subtle visual nod to the course's content (the curve). It's a branding element
intended to be appealing and representative of the course's introductory nature and focus on data.

Audio: Shark. You will receive the room assignment and see the assignment through email. Just watch off where that probably isn't going to come during the RR week. And then you should be able to know where you should show up 8 a.m. on the 18th. We're going to have review sessions. Review sessions are going to happen during the reading week in the same room at the same time as a lecture. So Tuesday and Thursday, 11 to 12, 30, same time. We're going to reserve Tuesday's review session to be post-meat term, because post-meat term is going to be the more focus of the final exam. Although the final exam is going to be cumulative, so it's going to have also the theme term stuff that the premium term topics are going to be reviewed on Thursday. So we have TAs who are running it, the capacity is going to be first on first serve, the recording is going to be released so that you can watch it afterwards if you can come to the class. You know that we have the course evaluation extra credit, I talked about it a few times before, if you fill it out, the campus evaluation and also our internal course evaluation, you will get 0.5% if 90% of the class, those of you will get how much home anymore. Another 25% super great deal given that the course is not careful so it's literally gonna bump your grade. So I'm gonna actually try to now wrap up what you've learned so when you take a look at the things that you actually have accomplished. And the topics that we covered is gonna be amazing. The goal that we had was to give you more advanced topics that you can now go ahead and take even more advanced topics in machine learning, statistics, data management, databases, and so on and so forth. The other goal is that to prepare you for carriers for internships, a lot of students are able to secure good internships right after data 100. So we talked about all of these technologies that we use in the course. You were able to actually work in Jupyter environment. We talked about pandas. So now you're very comfortable with the syntax of pandas. Video: 1: 0

Audio: This is gonna become really handy in your jobs. Now you know SQL a little bit, and now you're more comfortable actually learning more advanced topics and concepts and databases as well. We spend a lot of time in EDA phase. Remember that in EDA phase, we did a little bit of visualization. Now you know two or three very popular visualization libraries that you will use actually research and also in industry. We talked about Plotly, Seaborne, Matplotlib. We also use different techniques in order to make our visualizations a little bit better. We talked about, okay, now we can do principal component analysis. Now we can also actually visualize our model, visualize the trend in our model and actually see what our predictions are going to look like. We also remember that we were going around this cycle of, okay, we do EDA, we're not happy with the result of whatever we're visualizing and whatever we're seeing in the train of the data. So we're going to go back and do feature in generating and come back. We're going to do a little bit of regular expression. Video: 1: 0)

text:
Audio: We're going to do transformation. So all of those were kind of giving us the features that then we could use for the modeling phase that we actually did afterwards. Speaking of modeling, you learned really kind of building blocks of machine learning in data 100. You learn linear regression. We spend a lot of time talking about really the science, not necessarily the science, it is actually a science, but really the background of how we're creating a linear regression models and how we're evaluating these models and why we actually design all less in the way that we actually design. We also looked at the classification task. We looked at logistic regression. We have evaluated logistic regression. We looked at different metrics that you should actually consider when you're evaluating how the model is performing. We also a lot of it talked about clustering. So every time that in the, I think the second half of data 100 that we were looking at one specific kind of algorithm, my goal was to show you how easy it is now that you learn new algorithms. So don't be afraid of learning new algorithms, going through tutorials and just generally getting more familiar with different kinds of machine learning algorithms that are out there. One thing that sets you apart in data 100 from everyone else that's doing data science work is that now you're really comfortable with just knowing what's happening in behind this scene or in terms of like how these algorithms are designed. We spend a lot of time talking about model risk, model bias, model variance, and all of those things. And just understanding the implication of why is it important to do feature engineering, why is it important to just consider all different pieces of this kind of modeling pipeline that we did in terms of getting a quality output model. Now you understand that. Now when you're going into industry and you're building different models, you can actually argue for each one of those features that you have and you can actually have a better vision into how you can carry out the research. We talked about gradient descent. We use different kinds of libraries there. Video: 1: O

We did not talk about deep learning. Although I'm hoping that with today's lecture and also the last lecture, we gave you enough confidence to go ahead and explore it. text:
Audio: If you have any questions, you can actually ask those questions there and just generally build a community with your fellow students as well. Now, I think with all of these summary that I gave you, you should be able to kind of go and explore real-world applications. Either explore opportunities, explore different interest opportunities as well, or go ahead and find your own real-world project that you can work on. There are different resources where you can find different data sets, different competitions, different kinds of project ideas that you can go ahead and explore. And this is just a short list of very kind of Vellum-known kind of resources for work to get data says some are to find different data competitions that you can join. Of course, we always remind the students that everything that we talked about in Data 100, we tried to also introduce the idea of human context and ethics, true project B, true project A, also true homework tree. Every time that there was a data set that we could actually talk about human context and ethics, we tried to do that. But there is a separate course Data 104 that we suggest that you all take and kind of learn more about those concepts, more at depth. And again, you might think that you're very new to the discipline, but given the depths of the materials that you learn and the expertise that now you have in data science, I think you're amongst the most skilled people in the field. So use your power wisely. Think about the models that you're building. Think about their implications. Think about how you can interpret what's happening in your model and what you're making predictions on and what kind of models again you're building. So consider everything around the data and everything around this modeling pipeline and cycle that you're going to. Video: 1: More Course Advice and Recommendations

, 3: Real-world Applications
A great way to strengthen your knowledge of the ideas you learned in this class (and
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
portfolio to help you find jobs!) is to use your new skills to analyze real-world data.
There are countless sources of data available on the internet.
Find one, load it into a notebook, and get to work!
Places to look for data and applications of data science:
Awesome Public Datasets GitHub repo.
Kaggle.
Google's dataset search.
Towards Data Science.
reddit.com/r/dataisbeautiful.
Five Thirty Eight.
Pudding
and so on!

We also had Sarah who led all of the questions that you were asking in lectures and making a slide of kind of possible for us to run during each one of those lectures. And Nish was there to help us facilitate those conversations, Melavika actually was the great infrexier. We had a lot of infrastructural issues which kind of led towards the second half of the course we had to like move some of the deadlines because we were not able to fix some of the infrastructure issues that we had. So she worked really late nights to make the infrastructure ready for all of the homeworks and projects that be of understanding to you. Abby led data scholars. I heard great things from data scholars, the students, that how much she enjoyed working with her and homework parties and discussions and so on and so forth. Then, uh, was mentoring our UCS ones. Um, so when you join the staff, uh, or of course staff, you will generally start from a UCS one position. And you will generally work with the UCS 2, who is actually trying to train you and mentor you in the course. Um, Rosen Jessica Lederg, uh, a draft project area, Brandon helped us create, um, exam resources for you, uh, and Sharrey was behind all the content and homeworks that you have seen in data 100, making sure that everything is tested and packaged for you properly. We also had Sarah who led all of the questions that you were asking in lectures and making a slide of kind of possible for us to run during each one of those lectures. And Nish led the student support. So anytime that the students needed kind of accommodation and there were like a special circumstances that we needed to address. And Nish was there to help us facilitate those conversations, Melavika actually was the great infrexier. We had a lot of infrastructural issues which kind of led towards the second half of the course we had to like move some of the deadlines because we were not able to fix some of the infrastructure issues that we had. So she worked really late nights to make the infrastructure ready for all of the homeworks and projects that be of understanding to you. Abby led data scholars. I heard great things from data scholars, the students, that how much she enjoyed working with her and homework parties and discussions and so on and so forth. Then, uh, was mentoring our UCS ones. Um, so when you join the staff, uh, or of course staff, you will generally start from a UCS one position. And you will generally work with the UCS 2, who is actually trying to train you and mentor you in the course. Um, Rosen Jessica Lederg, uh, a draft project area, Brandon helped us create, um, exam resources for you, uh, and Sharrey was behind all the content and homeworks that you have seen in data 100, making sure that everything is tested and packaged for you properly. And obviously, you've seen Sam's messages on Ed every time, reminding you of the logistics of the upcoming week and generally handling all the great logistics that we had.
